coredata.dc:title: No-interfered and visual evaluation of global warming impacts on phytoplankton-based copper bioavailability and then carbon sequestration
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Fengjiao
Su, Lingling
Du, Yanting
Li, Huali
Luo, Jiayi
Huang, Xuguang
Li, Shunxing
Zheng, Fengying
coredata.dc:description: Global warming has an increasingly serious impact on the ecological environment. Copper bioavailability plays an important physiological role in revealing the mechanism of carbon cycle, photosynthesis, and respiration. Here we reported a multifunctional carbon quantum dots fluorescence probe for no-interfered and visual determination of phytoplankton-based intracellular Cu(II), glucose, and reactive oxygen species (ROS). Glucose and ROS were explored to reflect the change in primary biomass and carbon sequestration. H2O2 is acted as the standard material of ROS, and the fitting parameter for glucose and H2O2 concentrations was 0.42(r = 0.9972). Both glucose, ROS, and Cu2+ detection have advantages of wide linear range (24.8–3.96 × 105 μg/L, 6–9.6 × 105 ng/L and 5–15 × 103 nmol/L, respectively), high precision (1.22 %, 6.38 %, and 7.37 %, respectively), and low detection limit (86.7 ng/L, 5.32 ng/L, and 0.367 nmol/L, respectively). Cu2+ uptake was increased with the increasing of temperature, and the copper bioavailability in increasing order was Cu-PorPhyr > Cu-phthalate > Cu-EDTA. There were significant positive correlation between glucose and Cu2+(r = 0.9943). Copper bioavailability would directly affect the carbon sequestration, i.e., when the concentration of intracellular copper increases by 1 mg/L, the content of intracellular glucose increases by 412 mg/L approximately, equally to 2.47 g/L of carbon dioxide was fixed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Global warming
Copper bioavailability
Carbon quantum dots
Phytoplankton
Multifunctional fluorescence probe

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Stability analysis of CDs in phytoplankton
Determination of intracellular ROS and glucose
Stability, interference, and toxicity analysis of CDs in phytoplankton
Analysis of intracellular ROS and glucose
Analysis of copper complexes bioavailability by phytoplankton
Analytical performance of the carbon quantum dots
Influence of global warming on intracellular glucose and copper bioavailability by phytoplankton

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Biomarkers of CDs in phytoplankton (left, Chlorella vulgaris; right, Thalassiosira weissflogii).
Schematic illustration of the ROS and glucose sensor based on H2O2-sensitive SA-CDs.
Calibration curve CDs reacted with intracellular H2O2(a), glucose(b), and copper (c).
Influence of temperature and illumination on free copper ion bioavailability by fluorescence intensity of labeled algae.
Influence of temperature and illumination on copper-EDTA bioavailability by fluorescence intensity of labeled algae.
Influence of temperature and illumination on copper-phthalic acid bioavailability by fluorescence intensity of labeled algae.
Influence of temperature and illumination on copper-porphyrin bioavailability by fluorescence intensity of labeled algae.
The relationship between the concentration of intracellular Glucose and copper.

head.ce:title: No-interfered and visual evaluation of global warming impacts on phytoplankton-based copper bioavailability and then carbon sequestration

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Pollution Monitoring and Control, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou 363000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Pollution Monitoring and Control

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Pollution Monitoring and Control, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou, China, 363000

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science and Separation Technology, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou 363000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science and Separation Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Environment, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou 363000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Environment, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou, 363000, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Environment, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou 363000, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Environment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Global warming has an increasingly serious impact on the ecological environment. Copper bioavailability plays an important physiological role in revealing the mechanism of carbon cycle, photosynthesis, and respiration. Here we reported a multifunctional carbon quantum dots fluorescence probe for no-interfered and visual determination of phytoplankton-based intracellular Cu(II), glucose, and reactive oxygen species (ROS). Glucose and ROS were explored to reflect the change in primary biomass and carbon sequestration. H2O2 is acted as the standard material of ROS, and the fitting parameter for glucose and H2O2 concentrations was 0.42(r = 0.9972). Both glucose, ROS, and Cu2+ detection have advantages of wide linear range (24.8–3.96 × 105 μg/L, 6–9.6 × 105 ng/L and 5–15 × 103 nmol/L, respectively), high precision (1.22 %, 6.38 %, and 7.37 %, respectively), and low detection limit (86.7 ng/L, 5.32 ng/L, and 0.367 nmol/L, respectively). Cu2+ uptake was increased with the increasing of temperature, and the copper bioavailability in increasing order was Cu-PorPhyr > Cu-phthalate > Cu-EDTA. There were significant positive correlation between glucose and Cu2+(r = 0.9943). Copper bioavailability would directly affect the carbon sequestration, i.e., when the concentration of intracellular copper increases by 1 mg/L, the content of intracellular glucose increases by 412 mg/L approximately, equally to 2.47 g/L of carbon dioxide was fixed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Excessive CO2 emission in the atmosphere plays a key factor to global warming (Bezerra et al., 2024; Brune et al., 2017; Tang et al., 2024). Future increases in carbon dioxide will have a stronger warming effect on the climate than similar increases in the past (Haozhe He et al., 2023). Global warming has an increasingly serious impact on the ecological environment, and 93 % of its heat was absorbed by the ocean(Johnson and Lyman, 2020). Several sea subsurface temperature distributions having vary substantially (e.g., El Niño (Johnson and Birnbaum, 2017), the Pacific Decadal and North Atlantic Oscillation(Kenigson et al., 2018; Wills et al., 2019), and the South Pacific(Roemmich et al., 2016)). Recent studies predict that the average temperature will increase by 0.2 °C per decade over the next 20 years, and by 1.8 °C–4 °C up to 2100 (Assan et al., 2020; Sulukan et al., 2023). Rising temperature is one of the main reasons for the extinction of marine organisms(Song et al., 2019), and the change even may exceed the adaption of many organisms in current and potential future tolerances. Meantime, marine ecosystem's structure and productivity could be controlled by trace metals bioavailability (Morel et al., 2003; Morel and Price, 2003). Ocean warming can impact the distribution, growth, and physiological rates of many organisms as well as the nutrient supply. Ocean warming can decrease the primary production and negatively impact on the higher trophic levels, because of the potential shift in the distribution and bioavailability of essential trace metals (e.g., copper, iron, and zinc)(Lotze et al., 2019). Phytoplankton is the major primary producer in marine ecological environments, especially in oligotrophic waters, which account for more than 80 % of primary productivity(Liu et al., 2023; Pieterse et al., 2009). They occupy an important role in the supporting climate regulation services and marine ecosystem, which are directly related to the three main functions of the ecosystem (energy flow, material circulation, and information transfer)(Hatje et al., 2022; Peng et al., 2012).

Glucose is one of the main products of photosynthesis and source for cellular respiration. Glucose and its derivatives are also important structural components for organisms(Shendurse and Khedkar, 2016). So, glucose detection is great significance for carbon cycle, life science and biology, its concentration also can be an important marker for photosynthesis (Pieterse et al., 2009). Several plant-derived bioactive molecules are signal molecules used to regulate the plant's growth, development and reproduction, and the interaction of plants with the external environment (Pieterse et al., 2009). The study of these bioactive molecules in cells provides basic understanding for their ecological functions (Pieterse et al., 2009).

GEOTRACES has placed on the integration of metals biogeochemistry and ecosystems research for understanding the impacts of global change and the role of the ocean in the earth system (Group, 2007). The biogeochemistry cycle of trace metals depends on their bioavailability, such as the uptake, transport, expulsion, regeneration and transport of organisms in the food chain. Therefore, the trace metals uptake by phytoplankton are important part of the biogeochemistry cycle. Meanwhile, copper is an important cofactor in photosynthesis and respiration of algae, which affects the source and sink of atmospheric carbon. Most copper exists in the complex state in water, and is mainly directly utilized by algae in the ionic state(Kong and Price, 2022; Wang, 2011). At present, the most direct and effective method for evaluating copper bioavailability is periodic culture-absorption test, which takes a long time (at least 4 days) and is complicated to pretreat.

In conclusion, it is great significance to global climate change by exploring the correlation between phytoplankton biomass, photosynthetic glucose, copper bioavailability and carbon sequestration. However, the existing detection methods for them still have some disadvantages, such as complex pretreatment, long cycle, high cost and unsuitable for in vivo detection. There is an urgent need to develop a fast, simple, in situ, no-interfered, and selectively determined method in phytoplankton.

Fluorescence sensor is one of the most commonly used methods for rapid detection, especially the semiconductor fluorescent quantum dots have many excellent fluorescence properties, such as broad excitation spectrum, narrow emission spectrum and its wavelength can be controlled by grain size and composition, high quantum yield, good light stability (not easy to photobleaching) can achieve monadic excitation of multi-emission simultaneous labeling (Gao et al., 2004; Medintz et al., 2005; Smith and Nie, 2010). At present, the quantum dots used in biological tracing and detection mainly include cadmium selenide, cadmium zinc sulphide, indium zinc sulphide, indium copper selenide, carbon quantum dots and silicon quantum dots (Baker and Baker, 2010; Erogbogbo et al., 2008; Tang and Sargent, 2011). However, quantum dots containing heavy metals are generally biotoxic and biocompatibility, while silicon quantum dots are relatively complex and costly to prepare.

Carbon dots (CDs) has the advantage of stable fluorescence signal, small size, good water solubility, low biological toxicity, easy to large-scale synthesis and functional modification (Bhattacharya et al., 2017; Pieterse et al., 2009; Yang et al., 2024). Several studies have exploited CDs' fluorescence signal changes as a transduction mechanism probe (Pieterse et al., 2009). Most such applications have shown that aqueous CDs are capable to detect compounds and ions through interaction of the radicals with surface-modified functional groups (Majood et al., 2023; Pieterse et al., 2009). Therefore, in order to simplify the testing procedures and improve the sensitivity and specificity, here CDs is used as a real time, no-interfered, and visual multifunctional fluorescence detector to quantitatively determinate intracellular glucose, ROS, and Cu2+ concentration. A green and safe new technology for marine plankton-based copper bioavailability assessment to global change could be offered.

CDs were purchased from Beijing North Poly-State Science and Technology Co., Ltd. The fluorescence excitation and emission wavelengths appear at 343 and 440 nm. The morphology of the CDs was characterized by TEM (Fig. S1A), typical UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of the CDs were shown in Fig. S1B, IR spectroscopy was shown in Fig. S1C.

Thalassiosira weissflogii (CCMA-102) and Chlorella vulgaris (CCMA-410) were gotten from the State Key Laboratory for Marine Environmental Science, Xiamen University. Exponentially growing cells of C. vulgaris cells were cultured in seawater at different predicted sea surface temperature levels (20 °C, 25 °C, 30 °C)(Polovina et al., 2011; Sulukan et al., 2023). They were cultured in f/2 medium containing 15 mg/L CDs, under the light illumination of 140 μmol photons/(m2 s1) by a light: dark cycle as 14:10 h. The algal suspensions were stirred at 100 rpm to simulate the current of seawater and to reduce the adsorption of CDs by vessel. These experiments were triplicated (n = 3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability analysis of CDs in phytoplankton
ce:section.ce:para: After 6 days of culture, the labeled algal cells were centrifuged and washed three times with PBS buffer (pH = 7.4) to remove residual CDs adsorbed on the cell surface. TCS SP5 Laser Confocal Microscope was provided by Leica Microsystems (Germany) and used to observe cell labeling. The washed cells were in f/2 medium to continue culture, fluorescence change of CDs in culture medium was observed using to evaluate the stability of CDs in algal cells.

After 6 days of culture, the photosynthetic activity, chlorophyll a content, and survival rate were measured. The abundance of living cells was analyzed by a cell digestion assay(Agusti et al., 2006). Two samples of T. weissflogii and C. vulgaris were taken using to calculate total cell and living cell, respectively. DNAse I solution (200 μL) was added into living cell sample, heated in 37 °C water bath for 15 min, and then Trypsin trypsin solution (200 μL) was added and heated in a water bath at 37 °C for 30 min. The reaction was placed on ice and the reaction was terminated on ice for 5 min. The living cell density was counted by flow cytometry immediately. Survival rate = (living cell / total cell) × 100 %.

The two kinds of algae species were also cultured according to the above culture medium, followed by the addition of 1.0 mg/L algae self-extracted protein, and then regularly observed CDs fluorescence signal in the culture medium to determine whether the CDs was bind to protein into the cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of intracellular ROS and glucose
ce:section.ce:para: Synthesis of salicylic acid-modified carbon quantum dot (SA-CDs) was prepared by 1.0 mL CDs (3.0 g/L) and 0.0138 g of salicylic acid were added in a 100 mL volumetric flask, stirred 24 h in the dark, and then stored at 2 to 8 °C. UV spectra of CDs before and after mixed with salicylic acid was shown in Fig. S2.

The SA-CDs labeled algae cells were suspended in 500 mL of seawater, then incubated in a constant temperature magnetic stirrer at a constant temperature of 18 ± 0.5 °C and at a rotational speed of 100 r/min for 1 h under high pressure mercury lamp irradiation. Each sample was taken out and added into 20 μL FeCl3 (10 mmol/L), then the intracellular fluorescence signal was detected real-timely by TCS SP5 Laser Confocal Microscope (Emission wavelengths was at 495 nm).

To evaluate the sensitivity toward Cu2+ of the fluorescence probe, the specific metal ions (10 μmol/L), such as Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Ag+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, K+, Na+, Ca2+, and Mg2+, was investigated (Montillet et al., 2005). CDs solution and the metal ions solutions were equilibrated for 2 h before spectral measurements.

Porphyrin(Pro), phthalic acid(H2Lp), and EDTA were used as typical natural or synthetic organic complex in water environment. They were equilibrated 40 % ambient light with the ligand at a ratio of 1:1 with Cu2+ (1.0 mmol/L) for 2 d before being introduced into the reactional media (Song et al., 2014). 5 mL of Cu-Por, Cu-H2Lp, Cu-EDTA, and Cu2+ were taken to 10 mL colorimetric tubes, respectively. Fluorescence signals were measured every 5 min after adding CDs in order to evaluate the stability of CDs in these copper complexes solutions.

Two groups of CDs labeled algae were added into Cu-Pro, Cu-H2Lp, Cu-EDTA, and Cu2+, respectively. One was placed in the dark and the other was placed in xenon lamp for 2 h. The fluorescence intensity of CDs in algae cells was observed every 30 min. According to intracellular CDs fluorescence signal change (ΔF = F0-F1) to determine the degraded Cu2+ content, i.e., the bioavailability of copper complex could be evaluated. All the fluorescence intensity of CDs in algae cells was observed real-timely by TCS SP5 Laser Confocal Microscope (Emission wavelengths was at 495 nm). These experiments were triplicated (n = 3),

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability, interference, and toxicity analysis of CDs in phytoplankton
ce:section.ce:para: CDs could directly enter the phytoplankton cell through the membrane pore, and a clear fluorescence imaging can be observed, as shown in Fig. 1 . There was also found that the protein did not help to accelerate CDs into the algal cells (Fig. S3). The labeled algae continued to culture in f/2 medium, CDs could not be detected in the medium for 10 days (Fig. S4), which indicated that CDs could be stably present in the cells.

The biological interference and toxicity results were shown in Table S1. There was no significant difference in the photosynthetic activity and chlorophyll a content of T. weissflogii and C. vulgaris between the controlled and blank experiments. Moreover, the survival rates of T. weissflogii and C. vulgaris were 89.7 % and 94.5 %, respectively.

Therefore, CDs could directly into membrane hole and be observed clearly and stable fluorescence imaging in cytoplasm, which were almost no interfered to cellular physiological functions. Similarly CDs have strong fluorescence stability, and their fluorescence intensity is only reduced by 10 % to 20 % for a few hours under xenon lamp (Jaiswal et al., 2012). After laser irradiation for a few hours, no bleaching is observed and the fluorescence intensity is almost unchanged (Yu et al., 2005). Additionally, Xu et al. (2004) demonstrated that when the CDs concentration was less than 0.5 mg/mL, the HepG2 cell viability was 90 %–100 %, but more than 0.5 mg/mL, cell viability was reduced to 75 %, and this concentration has far exceeded the concentration required for biological imaging. Common tracer methods isotope tracing (radionuclide or stability nuclide) have high sensitivity and accurate positioning quantitative advantages, but there are still some shortcomings, especially isotope and radiation response. Moreover, tracer selection must be based on experimental purposes choosing effective and repeated determination of radioactivity, appropriate specific activity and half-life, and low radioactive isotopes. Therefore, CDs could be expected to become a new tracer in marine organisms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of intracellular ROS and glucose
ce:section.ce:para: Marine phytoplankton can photochemical product to ROS, which including singlet oxygen (1O2), superoxide radicals (O2 −), hydroxyl radicals (OH), and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). They can be readily reacted with oxygen or water in the environment and ultimately in the form of OH (Fig. S5), and H2O2 can be more effectively used to convert to OH, so H2O2 is usually acted as the standard material of ROS40.

H2O2 can react with benzene rings on the surface of SA-CDs to capture the electrons at the conduction bands of SA-CDs after adding into FeCl3, and subsequently the radiative recombination of photoinduced electrons and holes is inhibited. Therefore, the PL intensity of the SA-CDs decreases. Similarly, d-glucose is catalytically oxidized by glucose oxidase (GOx) to gluconic acid and H2O2. The fitting parameter for glucose and H2O2 concentrations was 0.42 (k = 0.42). There were significant positive correlation between glucose and H2O2(r = 0.9972). According to the change of fluorescence signal, the intracellular ROS and glucose content can be measured indirectly. Schematic illustration of the ROS and glucose sensor based on H2O2-sensitive SA-CDs is displayed in Fig. 2 .

CDs-SA could be sensitive to H2O2 and glucose in a certain concentration (Fig.3 ). Both glucose and ROS detection have advantages of wide linear range (24.8–3.96 × 105 μg/L and 6–9.6 × 105 ng/L, respectively), high precision (1.22 % and 6.38 %, respectively), and low detection limit (86.7 ng/L and 5.32 ng/L, respectively).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of copper complexes bioavailability by phytoplankton
ce:section.ce:para: As a fluorescence detectors, CDs has high selectivity specificity to Cu2+ (Fig.S6), because the reaction between Cu2+ and amino-group (−NH2) in CDs molecules in the formation of non-fluorescent complexes lead to rapid fluorescence quenching. So the concentration of Cu2+ could be detected by the fluorescence intensity change of CDs. The detection linear range, precision, and detection limit was 5–15 × 103 nmol/L, 7.37 %, and 0.367 nmol/L, respectively (Fig. 3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the practicability and accuracy of our multifunctional carbon quantum dots fluorescence probe, T. weissflogii and C. vulgaris are tested as real samples. The results and their comparisons with PeroxiDetect™ Kit assays (PDK), dinitrosalicylic acid (DNS) method, and ICP-MS are shown in Table. S2. There was no significant difference between the proposed and traditional method. Therefore, a no-interfered and visual technology for intracellular ROS, glucose, and Cu2+ detection could be offered, which has advantages of wide linear range, high precision, and low detection limit.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of global warming on intracellular glucose and copper bioavailability by phytoplankton
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence quenching of CDs in the two microalgae was only in the Cu2+ system could be found, indicating that only Cu2+ could through the membrane pore and induced CDs fluorescence quenching. After illumination, the copper complex was photolyzed to Cu2+, and then Cu2+ was into the cell to react with CDs (Fig. S7). The degradation rate of copper complex were Cu-Por > Cu-H2Lp > Cu-EDTA (Table S3 and Videos 1–3), i.e., the copper bioavailability in decreasing order was also Cu-Por > Cu-H2Lp > Cu-EDTA (Table S4 and Figs. 4–7). CDs fluorescence quenching and copper complex decomposition rate were Cu-Por > Cu-H2Lp > Cu-EDTA, which indicated that complex decomposition rate was related to the ability of light absorption (Por > H2Lp > EDTA) and stability constant (KcCu-Por (5.92) < KcCu-H2Lp (12–14) < KcCu-EDTA (20.57)). It also could further confirm that passive diffusion is the key way for metals to enter phytoplankton.

The fluorescence quenching of CDs in the two microalgae was only in the Cu2+ system could be found, indicating that only Cu2+ could through the membrane pore and induced CDs fluorescence quenching. After illumination, the copper complex was photolyzed to Cu2+, and then Cu2+ was into the cell to react with CDs (Fig. S7). The degradation rate of copper complex were Cu-Por > Cu-H2Lp > Cu-EDTA (Table S3 and Videos 1–3), i.e., the copper bioavailability in decreasing order was also Cu-Por > Cu-H2Lp > Cu-EDTA (Table S4 and Figs. 4–7). CDs fluorescence quenching and copper complex decomposition rate were Cu-Por > Cu-H2Lp > Cu-EDTA, which indicated that complex decomposition rate was related to the ability of light absorption (Por > H2Lp > EDTA) and stability constant (KcCu-Por (5.92) < KcCu-H2Lp (12–14) < KcCu-EDTA (20.57)). It also could further confirm that passive diffusion is the key way for metals to enter phytoplankton.

With the increase of temperature, we found that the intracellular glucose and copper were increased firstly and then decreased, and the significant positive correlation between them was 0.9943 (Fig. 8 ). When the concentration of intracellular copper increases by 1 mg/L, the content of intracellular glucose increases by 412 mg/L approximately, equally to 2.47 g/L of carbon dioxide was fixed. The results indicated that copper uptake could enhance the production of glucose, i.e., copper bioavailability would directly affect the carbon sequestration.

Metals toxicity, uptake, and bioaccumulation for several marine organisms are related to temperature (Marques et al., 2010).The rising temperature of aquatic ecosystems caused by global warming can directly affect the pH of water solution (Anderson et al., 2021; Kosten et al., 2012), and then the pH controls the metals bioavailability (Liu et al., 2016). Copper speciation, bioaccumulation, and bioavailability could control by interactive effects of temperature increase and environment physicochemical conditions (Bates et al., 2021; Hatje et al., 2022). Copper uptake and accumulation by marine organisms showed a positive relationship would be higher in a warmer ocean (Khan et al., 2006; Lan et al., 2020; Romero-Freire et al., 2020). Meanwhile, lots of previous studies have reported that global warming most likely will influence the uptake of copper to microalgae species (Gissela Pascual et al., 2022; Mesquita et al., 2023; Yong et al., 2021). We further observed that global warming might control copper bioavailability through its influence on its speciation, and copper bioavailability can also affect the concentration of glucose produced by photosynthesis, which in turn affects the amount of carbon dioxide fixed by phytoplankton. Our findings play a key role in understanding the effects of global change on metal biogeochemical cycle for GEOTRACES.

Fengjiao Liu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Resources, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Lingling Su: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Yanting Du: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Huali Li: Investigation, Data curation. Jiayi Luo: Investigation, Data curation. Xuguang Huang: Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Conceptualization. Shunxing Li: Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Conceptualization. Fengying Zheng: Resources, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (22074058 and 41976150), the Project of Industry-University-Research Cooperation of Fujian Province (2019Y4010).
